title: map
description: Key-value pairs, unique keys, automatically sorted by key — plus the classic gotcha where [] silently creates a missing entry.
objectives: Understand map as key-value pairs sorted automatically by key
Use find() and count() to check existence safely

sections.text: 1. Recap: set Stored Values; map Stores Pairs
set held a collection of unique values. map holds unique keys, each paired with an associated value — like a real dictionary, where a word (the key) maps to its definition (the value).
map stores key-value pairs with unique keys, automatically kept sorted by key, giving roughly logarithmic-time lookup by key.

This is one of map's most notorious surprises: reading ages["Ghost"] for a key that doesn't exist doesn't throw an error — it silently creates that key with a default value (0 for int) and returns that. Just checking whether a key exists using [] can accidentally insert it.

Recall count() from set — for map, it also only ever returns 0 or 1 (keys are unique), and unlike [], it never creates an entry as a side effect. find() works the same safe way, returning an iterator or end(). Checking existence with count() or find() rather than [] avoids the gotcha above.

auto and the range-based for loop are previews of upcoming topics. The key detail here: even though insertion order was Priya, Aman, Zara, the output comes out alphabetically by key (Aman, Priya, Zara) — map keeps itself sorted by key automatically, exactly like set does for values.
Iterating a map gives you a pair for each entry — pair.first is the key, pair.second is the value. This is a preview of the dedicated pair topic coming up, which explains this structure properly.

sections.title: A real, alphabetized dictionary
sections.text: A physical dictionary maps a unique word (key) to its definition (value), and every dictionary is naturally alphabetized (sorted by key) — exactly map's behavior. Looking up a word that isn't in the dictionary doesn't add a blank page for it automatically the way map[missingKey] silently does — that quirk is unique to map, worth remembering precisely because it's surprising.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Using [] to check whether a key exists — it silently creates the key with a default value if it doesn't; use count() or find() instead.
Expecting map to preserve insertion order — it's always sorted by key automatically.
Forgetting map keys must be unique — assigning to an existing key with [] updates its value rather than creating a duplicate entry.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What does map store, and how is it ordered?
questions.answer: Unique key-value pairs, automatically kept sorted by key.

questions.question: What happens when you access a non-existent key using []?
questions.answer: It's silently created with a default value (e.g. 0 for int) instead of throwing an error — a classic gotcha when using [] just to check existence.

questions.question: How should you safely check whether a key exists in a map?
questions.answer: Using count() (returns 0 or 1) or find() (returns an iterator or end()), neither of which inserts a new entry as a side effect, unlike [].

questions.question: What does each element look like when iterating a map?
questions.answer: A pair, where .first is the key and .second is the associated value.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: map stores unique key-value pairs, automatically kept sorted by key — accessed and inserted with [], like ages["Aman"] = 20;. A classic gotcha: reading a missing key with [] doesn't error, it silently creates that key with a default value, so count() or find() should be used instead to safely check existence without side effects. Iterating a map yields entries in sorted key order, each as a pair with .first as the key and .second as the value, giving roughly logarithmic-time performance for lookups, insertions, and removals.

sections.title: Trick to Remember map
sections.text: map = a real dictionary, word → definition, always alphabetized. [] on a missing key silently ADDS it — use count()/find() to just check.
